title.en: JSON vs YAML: Which Data Format Should You Use?

description.en: JSON vs YAML comparison — syntax, comments, anchors, performance, use cases in APIs and configuration, and when to use each format.

intro.en: JSON and YAML are both widely used data serialisation formats, and YAML is actually a superset of JSON (all valid JSON is valid YAML). Despite their similarities, they have very different use cases: JSON is the standard for APIs and machine-to-machine communication, while YAML is preferred for human-written configuration files due to its readability and comment support.

a.en: Universal API standard — every language has a JSON library
Fewer surprises — simple, predictable type system
Inline format readable for small objects

b.en: More human-readable for deeply nested structures
Anchors and aliases — reuse values without repetition
Multi-line strings are clean and readable
No brackets, commas, or quotes required in most cases

a.en: No comments — cannot document config files
No way to reference another value in the same document

b.en: Implicit typing causes bugs (yes → true, NO → false)
Indentation errors are hard to spot
More complex spec — harder to implement correctly

a.en: Use JSON for REST APIs, data storage, inter-service communication, browser localStorage, and any machine-to-machine data exchange where parsing speed and simplicity matter.

b.en: Use YAML for CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI), Kubernetes manifests, Docker Compose, Ansible playbooks, and any human-written configuration file where readability and comments are valuable.

performanceNote.en: JSON parsers are typically 2–10× faster than YAML parsers because JSON has a simpler grammar. For high-throughput APIs, this difference matters. For configuration files read once at startup, parsing speed is irrelevant.

a.en: Native browser support via JSON.parse() and JSON.stringify(). No external library needed.

b.en: Requires a library (js-yaml in Node.js/browsers, PyYAML in Python). No native browser support.

verdict.en: Use JSON for APIs and data exchange. Use YAML for configuration files that humans read and write. If your team is editing the file in a text editor and needs comments, YAML wins. If the file is generated by code and parsed by machines, JSON wins.

answer.en: Yes — all valid JSON is valid YAML. YAML is a strict superset of JSON from YAML 1.2 onwards. You can use a YAML parser to parse JSON files. The reverse is not true: YAML documents with comments, anchors, or YAML-specific syntax cannot be parsed by a JSON parser.

question.en: Can I use YAML in a REST API?

answer.en: Technically yes, but it is strongly discouraged. JSON is the universal API standard and has native support in every browser and HTTP client. Using YAML in APIs adds unnecessary complexity with no benefit over JSON for machine-to-machine communication.

question.en: How do I convert JSON to YAML?

answer.en: Use our JSON to YAML converter tool to convert instantly in your browser. In code: js-yaml (JavaScript), PyYAML (Python), or the yq command-line tool. Note that JSON has no comments, so the resulting YAML will also have no comments.
